person.name: Queen Euphemia Ross of Scotland

popularStory.headline: History of Euphemia de Ross (1322 - 1386)
popularStory.description:

Life

Euphemia was a daughter of Hugh, Earl of Ross, and Margaret de Graham, Hugh' …

person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
Euphemia de Ross (died 1386), a member of Clan Ross, was Queen of Scots as the second wife of Robert II of Scotland.

«b»Life«/b»
Euphemia was a daughter of Hugh, Earl of Ross and Margaret de Graham, Hugh's second wife and daughter of Sir John de Graham of Abercorn. She first married John Randolph, 3rd Earl of Moray but the marriage was childless. Her husband died in 1346 and she remained a widow for nine years.

On 2 May 1355, Euphemia married Robert Stewart, sole son of Walter Stewart, 6th High Steward of Scotland and Marjorie Bruce. Marjorie was a daughter of Robert I of Scotland and his first wife Isabella of Mar.

It appears that there was an obstacle of affinity to this second marriage, and a papal dispensation by Pope Innocent VI was required for it to be recognized by the Catholic Church. The affinity was due to her first husband, John Randolph, Earl of Moray, having been a second cousin of Robert Stewart. There also was a blood relationship, as the dispensation referenced their being related in the fourth degree of consanguinity, but this relationship has as yet not been identified. The children of Robert's first marriage to Elizabeth Mure were considered illegitimate by some due to reasons of consanguinity. Both sets of children from each marriage considered themselves rightful heirs to the throne, which constituted considerable future conflict.

Euphemia and Robert were parents to four children:

1.) David Stewart, 1st Earl of Caithness (d. bef. 1389)

2.) Walter Stewart, 1st Earl of Atholl (d. 1437)

3.) Elizabeth Stewart

4.) Egidia Stewart, married 1387 Sir William Douglas of Nithsdale

Robert II succeeded his childless maternal uncle David II of Scotland in 1371. Euphemia became his Queen and served in this position for about fifteen years, until her death in 1386.

sourceList.title: Euphemia De Ross Stewart, "Find A Grave Index"

zh.lifeSummary: 當Queen Euphemia Ross of Scotland於1329年出生在蘇格蘭艾斯爾吉爾馬爾諾可時，她的父親Hugo Dunkeld Earl van Ross54歲，母親Margaret Graham29歲。她於1355年5月2日與Robert II Stewart King of Scotland在蘇格蘭艾斯爾登東納爾德結婚。他們至少是2個兒子和2個女兒的父母。她去世於1386年2月20日在英國蘇格蘭艾斯爾登東納爾德享年57歲，埋葬在英國蘇格蘭雷福福爾佩斯利佩斯利 艾比。

nameMeanings.name: Queen
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from the Middle English female personal name Quene, sometimes a pet form of Quenill (Old English Cwēnhild, from cwēn ‘queen’ + hild ‘battle’). Early examples of the name are mainly from southern England, especially Sussex, but its currency in late medieval northern England has also been suggested by records from Yorkshire.
English: possibly a nickname from Middle English quene ‘woman; harlot; crone’ (Old English cwēn), but this is less likely than 2 to have given rise to a hereditary surname.

variants.name: McQueen
meanings: Scottish and Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Shuibhne ‘son of Suibhne’ a byname meaning ‘pleasant’. Compare McSweeney .

Scottish and Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Shuain, a Gaelic patronymic based on Old Norse sveinn ‘boy, servant’, which was also used as a personal name (see Swain ). This is given as the origin of the McQueen or McSwan family of Skye, although the spellings are hard to distinguish. Compare Whan .

nameMeanings.name: Euphemia
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: form of a Late Greek name derived from eu ‘well, good’ + phēnai ‘to speak’. This was the name of various early saints, notably a virgin martyr said to have been burned at the stake at Chalcedon in 307 . It had a limited popularity in some regions in the 16th and 17th centuries and returned to favour in the Victorian period, especially in the pet form Effie . It is little used at present.

nameMeanings.name: Ross
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Either an adoption of the Gaelic topographic term ros ‘headland’ ( compare Glen , Kyle ) or a transferred use of the Scottish surname, which is borne by a large and ancient family whose members have played a major role in Scottish history. Although still very popular in Scotland, the name is now in widespread general use.

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of Oswald or any of the various other names beginning with Os-.

nameMeanings.name: Scotland
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: from the Middle English personal name Scotland, Scolland. Found in Normandy as well as England, it evidently denoted a Scot or someone with an association with Scotland.
ethnic name for someone from Scotland, from Middle English Scotlond ‘Scotland’. Compare England .

variants.name: England
meanings: English: habitational name from Middle English Engelond ‘England’. It was probably a formal alternative to English , which is also well evidenced as a surname in England. These names may have been acquired by English landowners who moved in Norman social circles or who lived in a neighbouring country (Scotland, Wales, or Ireland), or by English merchants who traded abroad.
Norwegian: habitational name from any of various farmsteads so named, from Old Norse eng ‘meadow’ + land ‘land’.
Swedish: ornamental name with the same meaning as 2.

en.lifeSummary: When Queen Euphemia Ross of Scotland was born in 1329, in Kilmarnock, Ayrshire, Scotland, her father, Hugo Dunkeld Earl van Ross, was 54 and her mother, Margaret Graham, was 29. She married Robert II Stewart King of Scotland on 2 May 1355, in Dundonald, Ayrshire, Scotland. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 2 daughters. She died on 20 February 1386, in Dundonald, Ayrshire, Scotland, United Kingdom, at the age of 57, and was buried in Paisley Abbey, Paisley, Renfrewshire, Scotland, United Kingdom.

Elle a épousé Robert II Stewart King of Scotland le 2 mai 1355, à Dundonald, Ayrshire, Écosse. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 2 filles. Elle est décédée le 20 février 1386, à Dundonald, Ayrshire, Écosse, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 57 ans, et a été enterrée à Paisley Abbey, Paisley, Renfrewshire, Écosse, Royaume-Uni.

ja.lifeSummary: Queen Euphemia Ross of Scotlandが1329年にスコットランドエイルシュアイアキルマルノックで生まれた時，父Hugo Dunkeld Earl van Rossは54歳，母Margaret Grahamは29歳であった。1355年5月2日にスコットランドエイルシュアイアダンドナルドでRobert II Stewart King of Scotlandと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも2人の息子と2人の娘の親であった。彼女は1386年2月20日にイギリススコットランドエイルシュアイアダンドナルドで57歳で死去し，イギリススコットランドレンフリューシュアイアペイスリーペイスリー アビーにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Queen Euphemia Ross of Scotland(이)가 1329년 스컷랜드 에이르실르 킬마노크에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Hugo Dunkeld Earl van Ross(은)는 54세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Margaret Graham(은)는 29세였다. 그녀는 1355년 5월 2일 스컷랜드 에이르실르 던도널드에서 Robert II Stewart King of Scotland(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1386년 2월 20일 영국 스코틀랜드 에이르실르 던도널드에서 57세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 스코틀랜드 렌프레w실르 파이슬리 파이슬리 애브브이에 묻혔다.
